metadata.title: The Upside-Down Axis
metadata.notes: Calling Bullshit (Vox Style), film six — the shortest in the series (one transform). Florida Stand Your Ground 2005; 'only the graph's vertical axis is inverted — murders actually spiked' verified. NO date claimed for the graphic (transcript gives none); designer unnamed in narration; incompetence-not-malice per the book. Color law: crimson = the data fill (nod to the original), navy = the honest reading. Outro pads A09.

beats.narration_text: This is Liam for Humanitarians AI.
A news graphic once made the rounds: after Florida passed Stand Your Ground, gun murders looked like they fell off a cliff.
beats.tts_normalized_text: A news graphic once made the rounds: after Florida passed Stand Your Ground, gun murders looked like they fell off a cliff.

shot.note: cold open — a phone on a café table, a red chart glowing on screen (abstract shape, no legible text); shot.focus on the phone

beats.narration_text: Here is why The Upside-Down Axis belongs in your working knowledge. The systems, policies, and tools shaped by it affect decisions at your level — whether or not you understand the mechanism. Knowing the underlying logic gives you the frame; missing it means reasoning from output with no access to the inputs.
Same data, opposite story. This one isn't even subtle — once you see it, you can never unsee it.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Same data, opposite story. This one isn't even subtle — once you see it, you can never unsee it.

beats.narration_text: Here's the shape everyone saw: the line dives right after two thousand five. Law passes, murders plunge. Case closed.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Here's the shape everyone saw: the line dives right after two thousand five. Law passes, murders plunge. Case closed.

viz.note: recreation of the inverted graph: crimson-filled curve diving after 2005 (zero hidden at top for now)

beats.narration_text: Now read the vertical axis. Zero is at the top.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Now read the vertical axis. Zero is at the top.

beats.narration_text: On this chart, down means MORE murders. The plunge you saw is a spike.
beats.tts_normalized_text: On this chart, down means MORE murders. The plunge you saw is a spike.

viz.note: terracotta ring lands on the axis zero at the TOP; numbers grow downward

beats.narration_text: Flip the axis the way convention expects, and the same line rotates into the true story: after the law, gun murders rose.
beats.tts_normalized_text: Flip the axis the way convention expects, and the same line rotates into the true story: after the law, gun murders rose.

viz.note: THE beat: the whole plot rotates about its horizontal midline; the dive becomes the surge; fill shifts crimson→navy

beats.narration_text: The designer wasn't hiding anything — she preferred showing deaths downward, like dripping blood. Never assume malice where incompetence will do.
beats.tts_normalized_text: The designer wasn't hiding anything — she preferred showing deaths downward, like dripping blood. Never assume malice where incompetence will do.

card.title: NOT MALICE
card.sub: deaths drawn downward, like dripping blood

beats.narration_text: But conventions are load-bearing: up means more everywhere else, so your eye reads this chart wrong before your brain gets a vote.
beats.tts_normalized_text: But conventions are load-bearing: up means more everywhere else, so your eye reads this chart wrong before your brain gets a vote.

viz.note: three mini-charts (temperature, price, height) all up-means-more; the odd one out flagged

beats.narration_text: So before you believe any chart, read the axes. The picture is an argument — the axes are its fine print.
beats.tts_normalized_text: So before you believe any chart, read the axes. The picture is an argument — the axes are its fine print.

card.title: READ THE AXES FIRST
card.sub: the axes are the fine print
